title: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman dissects an anti-SB13 resolution filed at the Pittsburg County (OK) Republican Committee meeting on February 28, 2020, walking through each of its seven objections to argue that the pro-life establishment's stated reasons for opposing the abolition bill expose a fundamental commitment to regulating abortion rather than abolishing it.
audiences: Pro-life Republican voter sympathetic to rape/incest exceptions
Pastor weighing whether to endorse SB13
Constitutional conservative concerned about defying Roe
State legislator drafting abolition or pro-life legislation

questions.question: Why does the author insist that abortion exceptions for rape and incest are unjust rather than compassionate?
questions.audience: Pro-life Republican voter sympathetic to rape/incest exceptions
questions.answer: Silberman frames children conceived in rape as the most vulnerable class of human beings precisely because they are fatherless, and argues that allowing their execution punishes them for their father's crime — turning a 'compassion' clause into legalized predation on the defenseless.
questions.quote: Abolitionism is about upholding justice for the fatherless. No one is more fatherless than the child conceived in rape. This is probably the most vulnerable group of human beings on the planet. A law which allows for abortion in this situation is an atrociously evil law which makes the fatherless prey (See Isaiah 1:16-17; 10:1-2). The law must prohibit the execution of the child for the crime of his or her father.

questions.question: How does SB13 handle situations where the mother's life is threatened by pregnancy complications?
questions.audience: State legislator drafting abolition or pro-life legislation
questions.answer: Rather than carving out a blanket life-of-the-mother exception (which Silberman warns would be exploited as a loophole), SB13 protects doctors from murder liability when they have exhausted every option to save both lives — preserving the physician's role as healer while still treating both mother and child as persons.
questions.quote: As for the life of the mother, SB13 specifies that the doctor cannot be held liable for murder if they did everything they could to save both lives. It does not and should not have a blanket life of the mother exception. That would be a loop hole that would be abused severely. Doctors are supposed to be healers, not killers, and SB13 specifies that they must exhaust all options at their disposal to save both human beings in cases of pregnancy complications demanding triage.

questions.question: Why must existing pro-life statutes — like dismemberment bans and parental notification laws — be repealed for abortion to actually be abolished?
questions.answer: Silberman argues these regulatory statutes implicitly codify the legality of abortion by specifying which methods, locations, providers, and notification procedures make the killing acceptable; leaving them on the books would directly contradict any law that treats abortion as murder.
questions.quote: All of these laws passed by pro-life politicians and lobbyists regulate where babies can be murdered, how they can be murdered, by whom they can be murdered, and the licensing needed  to murder them. Such laws explicitly contradict SB13 which abolishes abortion as murder. **Abortion cannot be abolished while there are still laws on the books saying abortion is legal as long as you jump through pro-life hoops.** They have to be repealed. SB13 repeals them.

questions.question: Why does the article reject classifying abortion as a 'health service'?
questions.audience: Pastor weighing whether to endorse SB13
questions.answer: Because abortion intentionally ends a human life, the author insists it cannot legitimately occupy any place in the health code and must be relocated to the criminal homicide statutes — where any other intentional killing is treated.
questions.quote: Abortion is not health care. It’s murder. Abortion must be taken out of the health codes and put into the criminal homicide codes. The pro-life author of the resolution appears to want abortion to continue to be legislated as if it were health care.

questions.question: How does the article respond to the objection that SB13 would expose mothers, nurses, and physicians to murder charges?
questions.audience: Pro-life Republican voter sympathetic to rape/incest exceptions
questions.answer: Silberman treats this not as a flaw but as the whole point of equal protection: granting preborn humans the same legal status as the born necessarily means anyone who kills or aids in killing them faces the same penalties — and exempting mothers, he argues, was the very hypocrisy that helped Roe succeed.
questions.quote: Put simply, all an abolition bill does is make preborn human beings completely equal under the law. This means that murdering or being an accomplice to the murder of a preborn human being comes with the same legal penalty as murdering or being an accomplice to the murder of a born human being. Pro-life bills which give automatic legal immunity to the mother deprive preborn human beings of equal justice and protection. This hypocrisy of treating the murder of a preborn human being differently than the murder of a born human being actually led to the decriminalization of abortion in the first place.

questions.question: How does footnote 54 of Roe v. Wade figure into the argument against automatic immunity for mothers?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative concerned about defying Roe
questions.answer: Silberman cites Justice Blackmun's own reasoning that the Texas statute's blanket immunity for mothers proved Texas didn't actually regard the preborn as persons; abolitionists therefore see equal penalties as essential not just morally but to legally rebut the Roe rationale.
questions.quote: The Texas abortion statutes in question in *Roe* gave automatic immunity to the mother in all cases. Justice Blackmun, author of the *Roe* ruling, wrote in footnote 54 of the decision that if Texas really believed preborn human beings were persons deserving of legal protection, the state would legally penalize those who murder them to the same degree as those who murder born persons.

questions.question: What does the rise of abortion-pill technology have to do with whether mothers should receive automatic immunity?
questions.audience: State legislator drafting abolition or pro-life legislation
questions.answer: The author warns that exempting mothers effectively legalizes self-induced abortion at home, a practice growing more common as chemical-abortion technology spreads — making 'mother immunity' a hollow ban that can be circumvented by mail-order pills.
questions.quote: Another enormous problem with providing automatic immunity to the parents who murder their preborn child is the fact that it creates a right to self-induced abortion, a practice which is increasingly common as abortion pill technology advances. **To write a law outlawing abortion but giving automatic immunity to the parents means that the mother is free to induce her own abortion.** SB13 does not fail to provide equal protection to preborn humans and therefore does not legalize self-induced abortion.

questions.question: On what grounds does the article justify a state defying Roe v. Wade and federal court orders?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative concerned about defying Roe
questions.answer: Silberman invokes both constitutional fidelity and the doctrine of the lesser magistrate, contending that orders to permit mass killing have no rightful authority and that state officials are morally obligated to interpose themselves between the federal judiciary and the unborn.
questions.quote: *Roe* is an evil, unconstitutional supreme court opinion ordering the states to allow mass murder within their jurisdictions. An abolition bill recognizes that the courts have no rightful authority to legalize murder. Court opinions legalizing murder are null and void by their nature and will be treated as such upon the passage and enforcement of an abolition bill. In addition to a magistrate’s duty to uphold the Constitution in the face of judicial tyranny, all men have a transcendent, immutable moral obligation to disregard an order from a superior to allow mass murder, which is exactly what *Roe* is. Lower magistrates must interpose themselves between the victims of abortion and any higher magistrate who would deprive preborn human beings of the right to life.

questions.question: Why does the article point to Alabama's 2019 abortion law and a new Planned Parenthood megacenter as a cautionary tale?
questions.answer: Because Alabama's bill explicitly invited court review rather than nullifying Roe, Planned Parenthood was confident enough to break ground on a Birmingham megacenter — proof to Silberman that bills lacking nullification language will simply be enjoined and never enforced.
questions.quote: As an example of a bill that lacks language nullifying *Roe*, in 2019, Alabama passed a court-submissive bill establishing that it’s illegal for an abortionist to perform abortion. Yet Planned Parenthood is so confident the court will not allow the law that they broke ground on a new megacenter in Birmingham a few months after the bill passed. SB13 does not submit to *Roe* or any other government edict purporting to legalize abortion.

questions.question: What does Silberman ultimately diagnose as the root problem with the resolution's 'We do support pro-life' closer?
questions.audience: Pastor weighing whether to endorse SB13
questions.answer: He argues that the regulatory pro-life strategy has cultivated a class of activists who are now more comfortable managing abortion than ending it — the same posture he attributes to Oklahoma Republican leaders Greg Treat and Kim David — and calls for jettisoning that mindset entirely.
questions.quote: Here’s the kicker. This person is a pro-lifer, through and through, who pays lip service to the idea that abortion is bad. This resolution is being introduced at a Republican Committee Meeting. Like Greg Treat and Kim David and the Republican establishment, the resolution’s author supports pro-life regulations on abortion but opposes actually establishing justice for preborn human beings. And that’s the fruit of a strategy of regulating abortion – many pro-lifers are now comfortable with regulating murder and uncomfortable with abolishing it. We’ve got to leave behind entirely this mindset, this political strategy of regulating abortion. Abortion must be utterly abolished.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
related.reason: Lays out the strategic case for refusing to support any Oklahoma bill weaker than SB13, providing the broader rationale for the article's defense against the Pittsburg County resolution.

related.title: Anti-SB13 Campaigners Aren't Ignorant. They're Trying to Deceive You.
related.reason: Companion piece arguing that the misrepresentations of SB13 — like those embedded in the Pittsburg County resolution — are willful rather than honest mistakes.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Names the establishment figures (including Greg Treat) the article invokes as exemplars of the pro-life mindset blocking SB13.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Extends the article's critique of Treat, whom Silberman links directly to the same pro-life regulatory posture as the resolution's author.

related.title: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
related.reason: Documents the legislative obstruction of SB13 happening in parallel with the county-level GOP resistance described here.

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
related.reason: Counterweight to the Pittsburg County GOP resolution: an Oklahoma faith body publicly aligning with the abolitionist position SB13 embodies.

related.title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
related.reason: Earlier example of an abolitionist rebutting institutional opposition to SB13 — same rhetorical move applied to clergy rather than party officials.
